voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Philippines — rivers, mangroves, and forested riverbanks
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: A small third eye on its head senses changes in light level.
Its eyes give it sharp color vision for spotting fruit, leaves, and movement.
Its tongue picks up chemical particles to identify food and other lizards.
It feels water currents and vibrations through its body while swimming.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives along rivers, streams, and mangrove edges on the Philippine islands.
It basks in trees above the water and drops into the river when threatened.
Dense riverbank vegetation provides food, basking sites, and cover.
It needs freshwater access for escape, cooling, and breeding.
How does it send messages?: Males raise their large sail-like dorsal crest to signal dominance to other males.
Males turn a darker, more vivid color during mating season.
Head bobbing and push-up displays communicate territorial ownership.
It drops from a branch into the water as a clear signal of alarm.
What does it do when something changes?: When a predator approaches, it drops from its branch directly into the water.
It can hold its breath underwater for up to fifteen minutes to avoid detection.
Young lizards can run briefly across the water surface when fleeing danger.
After a disturbance, it surfaces quietly and stays low near the bank.
How does it learn?: Hatchlings are independent from birth and must learn foraging and escape on their own.
Juveniles stay near riverbanks, learning which plant patches and perches are safest.
Young lizards develop their ability to swim and dive through repeated practice.
How does it have babies?: A female digs a nest near water and lays two to eight eggs.
Eggs incubate in the warm soil for about sixty days before hatching.
Hatchlings dig out of the nest and head immediately toward water.
A female can produce several clutches in a single year.
What are its limits?: Without permanent freshwater nearby, it has no escape route.
Loss of riverbank trees removes its basking and shelter sites.
Hunting, the pet trade, and river habitat loss put it at risk.
It needs warm temperatures year-round as a cold-blooded reptile.
What does this teach us?: It uses both tree canopy and river as one connected home.
Its crest signals rank, aids swimming, and helps control body heat.
The river is a refuge, a travel route, and a place to hide.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Has a functional parietal ('third') eye on the top of its head used for light sensing.
Drops from basking branches directly into the river to escape predators.
Juveniles can briefly run across the water surface.
Endemic to the Philippines and tied to freshwater river systems.
still_debated: How long individuals can actually remain submerged without stress.
The exact signaling function of the male's sail crest beyond dominance.
Population trends in river systems that receive intermittent protection.
common_failure_modes: Calling it an iguana — it is an agamid, different family on a different continent.
Treating all 'sailfin' lizards as one species.
Ignoring its strict freshwater requirement when designing off-exhibit habitat.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe it as fully terrestrial; it requires a river or equivalent water source.
Do not call it an iguana.
Do not describe juvenile water-running as a long-distance behavior — it is brief.
